original_abstract: While mainstream scholarship often contends that Islam has had little impact on Indonesia’s foreign policy, the nation’s diplomatic history during its revolution for independence suggests otherwise. Between 1945 and 1949, the decentralized nature of the independence struggle allowed non-state actors and civil society to play a direct role in conducting foreign relations. During this pivotal period, Indonesia leveraged Islamic identity as a strategic cornerstone of its diplomatic outreach to the Arab world. While this approach secured crucial early recognition and international support, shifting geopolitical dynamics after 1948 eventually marginalized the role of Islam, leading to a decline in the prominence of Arab-Indonesian relations in the country’s foreign policy agenda.
humanized_abstract: Mainstream accounts of Indonesian foreign policy have tended to suggest that Islam has played little part in shaping Jakarta’s international conduct. Yet an exploration of Indonesia’s diplomatic history during the country’s struggle for independence from Dutch colonial rule between 1945 and 1949 suggests otherwise. It is argued here that the decentralized nature of the struggle for Indonesian independence provided unprecedented opportunities for non-state and civil society organizations to engage in foreign policy activities. The official diplomatic history of Indonesia also confirms that the new nation made considerable use of the Islamic dimension of its international identity in order to cultivate good relations with the Arab world. Early international recognition of Indonesia was secured for the new republic with the support of Arab and Muslim countries, and Indonesia’s involvement in the Islamic Movement for Pakistan following India’s decision to oppose Pakistan’s membership of the United Nations also provided further support to the Indonesian government’s campaign for independence. However, as Indonesian diplomacy became more formally institutionalized and diplomatic relations with other Western and non-Communist countries developed more rapidly after 1948, the salience of Islam and of Indonesian–Arab relations in Indonesian foreign policy began to decline.

Early international recognition of Indonesia was secured for the new republic with the support of Arab and Muslim countries, and Indonesia’s involvement in the Islamic Movement for Pakistan following India’s decision to oppose Pakistan’s membership of the United Nations also provided further support to the Indonesian government’s campaign for independence. However, as Indonesian diplomacy became more formally institutionalized and diplomatic relations with other Western and non-Communist countries developed more rapidly after 1948, the salience of Islam and of Indonesian–Arab relations in Indonesian foreign policy began to decline.
